data.title: Live updates: Trump to address World Economic Forum as he digs in on Greenland
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday morning will speak at the World Economic Forum, where he’ll face off with European leaders amid his push to seize Greenland.

Here’s what to expect from the president’s trip to Davos, Switzerland, as some Trump allies on Capitol Hill suggest the president should back off his efforts to acquire the island nation.

Trump arrived in Switzerland earlier this morning after an electrical issue forced Air Force One to turn back to Joint Base Andrews to board a new aircraft.

In Washington, the Supreme Court will hear oral arguments in the case regarding Trump’s firing of Federal Reserve board member Lisa Cook. Here’s what you need to know.

The House on Wednesday, meanwhile, will have votes to hold former President Clinton and Secretary of State Hillary Clinton in contempt, as Republicans seek to shift the heat over the Jeffrey Epstein files away from the conference.

Congress is also staring down a Jan. 31 government shutdown deadline as Democrats threaten to derail Department of Homeland Security funding negotiations over recent federal immigration enforcement action in Minnesota.

Follow live below.

data.title: Watch live: Trump gives speech in Davos amid Greenland push
data.description:
President Trump will give a speech Wednesday morning at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland — bringing him face-to-face with European allies as tensions build over his push to acquire Greenland.

While Trump is likely to tout how his policies have made the U.S. economy stronger, the high-stakes meetings could play a significant role in determining the future of Washington’s involvement in the Danish territory. He is expected to talk with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte and other allies during his trip, after threatening over the weekend to slap tariffs on nations that do not support his administration’s takeover of the Arctic island.

The president is also waiting for answers from many world leaders on who will join his Board of Peace in Gaza.

The address is scheduled to begin at 8:30 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Levitt jokes Qatari plane sounding 'much better' after Air Force One tech issue
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt joked to reporters that the Qatari plane gifted to the U.S. was sounding “much better” following a tech issue that forced Air Force One to return to Joint Base Andrews on Tuesday. 

The incident took place as President Trump was en route to Davos, Switzerland for the World Economic Forum. 

Leavitt confirmed that a “minor electrical issue” was found after takeoff and, out of an abundance of caution, the plane returned to JBA.

Trump, his staff and the press pool then got on a smaller plane, later landing in Zurich early Wednesday afternoon, local time

Trump is expected to give a speech at Davos and meet with a host of foreign leaders on matters concerning Greenland and the creation of a Board of Peace intended to oversee Gaza.

Trump accepted the gift jet, a $400 million luxury Boeing 747-8 that the Qatari royal family had previously used. Dubbed a “flying palace” the gift caused controversy at the time with critics saying it violated the emoluments clause and presents conflicts of interest.

The plane does not appear to currently be in use. Chief Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell previously said the Department of Defense will “work to ensure proper security measures and functional-mission requirements are considered for an aircraft used to transport the President of the United States.”

data.title: Trump's unhinged Norway letter is a danger to national security
data.description:
President Trump’s letter this week to Norway’s prime minister — essentially demanding the handover of Greenland by a third NATO country, Denmark — is not merely inappropriate and undignified. It is incoherent, legally reckless, and hugely destabilizing to an alliance critical to our security.

No previous president of either party could have written such a document. The letter reads like a stream of grievances, misconceptions, and threats. It seriously calls into question whether Trump understands the powers and responsibilities of his office.

It begins with the extraordinary claim that because Norway “decided not to give me the Nobel Peace Prize for having stopped 8 Wars PLUS,” Trump longer feels “an obligation to think purely of Peace.” He may now focus instead on “what is good and proper” for the U.S.

Every part of this sentence is troubling. Norway does not even decide who receives the Nobel Peace Prize; an independent committee does. The prize is not a transactional reward owed personally to Trump as a bargaining chip. The claim of having stopped eight wars or more is also unsubstantiated and unrecognized by any serious international body.

More disturbing still is the implication that American restraint and responsibility are contingent upon other diplomats’ aggrandizement of Trump.

The suggestion that he no longer feels obligated to “think purely of Peace” reads like a threat. The implication that, until now, he had not pursued what is good and proper for America is true, but presumably not intended — it occupies the dark space between admission and absurdity.

Turning to Greenland, Trump then writes that Denmark “cannot protect that land from Russia or China,” and questions why Denmark has a “right of ownership” just because “a boat landed there hundreds of years ago.” He adds that “we had boats landing there, also.”

This passage collapses under even minimal scrutiny. It falsely implies that Denmark stands alone in defending Greenland. In fact Greenland is part of the NATO security umbrella, and any attack on it would engage the collective defense obligations of the alliance, including the U.S. It reduces sovereignty to military capability, suggesting that the right to territory flows from who can “protect” it, rather than from law, governance, international recognition, and the will of its people.

That logic mirrors the very arguments the U.S. rejects when advanced by Russia in Ukraine or by China in the South China Sea. If sovereignty is determined by power rather than law, then America forfeits its moral and legal basis for opposing territorial aggression elsewhere. The damage invites precisely the behavior Washington claims to resist.

The letter also ignores Greenland’s actual status. It is not a colonial outpost but an autonomous territory, with its own elected government and explicit rights of self-determination.

Trump then declares that he has “done more for NATO than any person since its founding” and that now “NATO should do something for the United States.” This is historically indefensible. NATO is the product of decades of collective effort by American and European leaders alike. Trump himself has repeatedly weakened the alliance, calling it “obsolete,” questioning its mutual defense obligations, and now, astoundingly, refusing to rule out using force against a member state. The suggestion that NATO owes him personally is just that much more absurd.

Besides, NATO already “does something” for the U.S., which currently operates or has access to 27 military bases in NATO countries — bases that former Supreme Allied Commander Chris Cavoli, an American general, once called “the first line of defense of our homeland.”

There has also been a huge rise in real annual defense spending by non-U.S. alliance members, from $289 billion in 2014 to $609 billion in 2025. Data from the 2020 to 2024 period also indicates that 64 percent of arms purchases by European NATO members came from U.S. firms.

NATO’s mutual defense clause, Article Five, has been invoked only once in history — for the U.S. after 9/11, freeing up NATO assets for the theaters in Iraq and Afghanistan.

Despite Trump’s rhetoric, Americans seem to grasp all of this. In recent polling, 68 percent said they supported the alliance.

Trump’s letter culminates in perhaps its most extraordinary claim: “The World is not secure unless we have Complete and Total Control of Greenland.” No serious strategic doctrine supports this idea. The U.S. does not control every region vital to global stability, nor could it.

Security comes from alliances, law, deterrence, and cooperation, not possession. Greenland is already inside a collective defense system that includes the U.S. America already has extensive military access there through agreements with Denmark.

The letter’s placement of personal grievance above national interest, its erratic capitalization, breathless tone, self-referential language, and its performative signoff (“Thank you! President DJT”) all project not authority but instability. As for its logic, such as it is, it may even embolden China and Russia and legitimize their future aggressions.

It is for moments like this that the Twenty-Fifth Amendment exists. We’re probably not there yet, but soon even Republicans in the Cabinet and Congress may have to start thinking about putting national interest first.

Colonel (Ret.) Robert Hamilton is president of the Delphi Global Research Center and former head of Eurasia Research at the Foreign Policy Research Institute. Dan Perry is the former chief editor of the Associated Press in Europe, Africa and the Middle East, former chairman of the Foreign Press Association in Jerusalem, and the author of two books.

data.title: Don't just increase proteins in the Food Pyramid — help make them healthier
data.description:
On Jan. 7, the U.S. Department of Agriculture and the Department of Health and Human Services released its Dietary Guidelines for Americans for 2025 through 2030. Secretaries Brooke Rollins and Robert F. Kennedy Jr. billed the rollout as “the most significant reset of federal nutrition in our nation’s history.”

In some respects, they’re right. The new guidance is clear: Eat real food. Cut added sugar. Avoid ultra-processed products loaded with additives.

But the new food pyramid makes one high stakes change that could undercut its own promise. It urges Americans to eat substantially more meat and dairy, while declining to distinguish between animal products from healthy sources and those produced in industrial systems.

Putting steak and cheese above refined carbohydrates, generally speaking, sounds sensible. But without a shift in how animal agriculture works, increasing U.S. consumption of meat and dairy also means ramping up dangerous antibiotics, pesticide-saturated feed, and pollution-heavy waste systems.

The problem isn’t the focus on meat or dairy per se. The problem is where most of it comes from. In the U.S., much of our animal food supply depends on large confinement facilities — concentrated animal feeding operations built for maximum output at minimum cost. Producers rely on routine antibiotics and other drugs to keep disease in check, creating further risks of antibiotic resistance that threaten human health.

Feed often comes from corn and soy grown in pesticide-heavy monocultures and shipped in at scale. And the waste doesn’t disappear: manure from these concentrated animal feeding operations comes in volumes that can pollute local air, contaminate streams and groundwater, and degrade surrounding land. In Ohio, we saw huge algal blooms choke off aquatic life in Lake Erie that were directly tied to a major animal feeding operations in the northwest part of the state.

The unhealthy side of that production model shows up in the food, too. Animal products can carry what the system uses — chemical residues from feed and persistent pollutants that move through soil and water. Some contaminants concentrate in animal tissue, especially fat. When you eat higher on the food chain, you don’t just get more protein — you also absorb more of the system’s chemical footprint.

All this might seem like a niche concern for Whole Foods moms, but it isn’t. It is about what ends up in kids’ school meals. It’s about what farmworkers breathe and touch, and what runs off fields into streams and wells that provide our water. Farm states like Iowa have faced rapid rises in cancer rates in recent years, underscoring the risks of the chemical-intensive industrial system to the people who grow our food.  

If the federal government wants Americans to eat more animal-based foods, it should clarify the importance of decent standards. In some cases, that means organic or pasture-raised. But it can also mean a broad range of regenerative practices like cover-crops, diversified rotations, or managed grazing.

Of course, the problem is tat all of this is expensive, especially amid rising food prices that are squeezing Americans’ budgets.

That’s why a serious strategy to “make America healthy again” should focus on helping farmers reduce dependence on chemical inputs and increase affordable access to healthy, minimally-processed foods. It should reimagine the system so that any agricultural subsidy steers us toward wellness and ultimately reduces healthcare costs.

Rollins and Kennedy are touting their new initiatives around “regenerative agriculture,” but the Trump administration has also cut the capacity that helps farmers transition away from destructive industrial practices. There are fewer experts available now to help farmers plant cover crops, improve grazing, reduce chemical inputs, and rebuild soil health.

At the same time, Republicans in Congress have been seeking to shield pesticide companies from lawsuits and to weaken oversight of “forever chemicals” harmful to human health. And the Trump administration is staffing agencies, including the EPA, with lobbyists from the pesticide industry. 

In essence, the Trump administration is recommending that people eat healthy food while eliminating the rules and tools that make that possible. Policymakers need a better strategy. 

First, if guidelines recommend more meat and dairy, they should also say what kind: products raised with fewer chemicals, less routine drug use, and better land and water practices. Keep it simple: If you eat more animal foods, do your best to choose sources that reduce chemical exposure and pollution.

Second, make those choices cheaper and easier. The government already shapes markets through what it buys and funds. But the Agriculture Department’s purchases still overwhelmingly favor big industrial agriculture and help concentrate power among megaproducers. It should strengthen procurement standards, including for school meals and other public programs.

Third, align farm policy with nutrition goals. Shift subsidies and incentives away from chemical-dependent commodity production and toward diversified farms, regional processing, and supply chains that let small and mid-sized producers compete. 

American nutrition needed a reset. Inverting the food pyramid can be a positive development. But if we want a “real food” system, we need to invest in building one.

Tim Ryan is a former 10-term Democratic member of the U.S. House from Ohio. Justin Talbot Zorn served as legislative director for three members of Congress and is a senior adviser at the Center for Economic and Policy Research in Washington.

data.title: Trump threatens to upend GOP agenda, Senate race with Louisiana endorsement
data.description:
President Trump is giving Senate GOP leaders a major headache with his decision to endorse Rep. Julia Letlow’s (R-La.) Senate campaign over Sen. Bill Cassidy’s (R-La.), tossing fresh uncertainty over key parts of their agenda and upending the race in a red state.  

Trump preemptively backed Letlow’s Senate campaign over the weekend. The move roiled the contest for a safe GOP Senate seat, put him at odds with Senate GOP leaders and created fresh tumult for the party’s forthcoming legislative push. 

Headlining that effort would be a second partisan package passed with only GOP votes and a potential health care bill, both of which would likely run through the Health, Education, Labor and Pensions (HELP) Committee — which Cassidy chairs. This year could offer Republicans’ last chance to usher through a bill with only GOP support if the party loses the House in November. 

“Did anyone explain that to the president?” one GOP operative with knowledge of the Trump-Cassidy relationship asked. “Cassidy as a free agent is one of the scarier ones in the Senate Republican Conference.” 

The operative noted that the HELP panel not only features the two-term lawmaker, but also Sens. Susan Collins (R-Maine) and Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) — two members who are notoriously cool to the president’s wishes. 

“To get a majority with Dems on things to f— the president [on the committee], it’s not complicated,” they added. 

Trump has zeroed in on Cassidy for years, especially after the Louisiana Republican voted to convict him in his impeachment trial over his actions on Jan. 6, 2021. 

Cassidy, a physician, has worked to make amends and stay in Trump’s good graces after the president was reelected. He notably voted to confirm Robert F. Kennedy Jr. to lead the Department of Health and Human Services despite his reservations.

Cassidy was already facing several primary challenges from his right flank prior to Letlow’s entrance

GOP leaders are continuing to back the incumbent Republican. Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso (R-Wyo.) told reporters on Tuesday that he’s backed Cassidy “from the beginning” and that plan isn’t changing

“We’re going to do everything we can to build the majority,” Barrasso said when asked how much of a “headache” the president’s endorsement is for the party. 

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and the National Republican Senatorial Committee are also fully behind Cassidy, with Thune appearing in Louisiana alongside the senator for an event to promote the GOP’s tax cut bill and a fundraiser last Thursday. 

The appearance came a day before Trump phoned Thune to inform him of his plan to support Letlow, who won her seat in 2021 after her late husband passed away. Chatter had been percolating about her potential entrance dating back to the fall. 

According to a source familiar with the leader, Thune “did what he could do” to try to sway Trump to Cassidy’s side, including laying out the Louisiana senator’s accomplishments. He also pointed out the narrow margins Republicans have to work with on various bills and nominations and that “having someone like Cassidy on our side is important” to secure many of Trump’s priorities. 

A bipartisan group of lawmakers have been working toward a compromise bill to extend the enhanced ObamaCare tax credits, but they have run into a brick wall in recent weeks that is making members pessimistic that a deal can come together. 

GOP leaders have kept the door open to a second reconciliation package, with health care among the topics under consideration for inclusion — especially if a bipartisan deal does not come together. 

Republicans were also quick to note that Trump’s move complicates things for Thune, who has the already difficult task of keeping the chamber together heading into an election year. 

“We have seven or eight votes that are up for grabs on every bill. … It hurts Thune, which therefore hurts Trump because he’s the only one that protects Trump from some of these senators,” the GOP operative said. “You can’t expect John Thune to deliver anything when all you’re doing is attacking his members. At some point, they don’t listen to John Thune because you’re attacking them anyway.” 

The majority of the Senate GOP doesn’t share Trump’s feelings about Cassidy, who is held in high regard across much of the conference, one senior GOP aide noted. 

“It’d be a little different if he was one of the more unpopular guys, but that’s not the case,” they said.

However, multiple Republicans indicated that they believe they have time before Cassidy becomes a potential legislative impediment. The Louisiana primary is not until mid-May, meaning that the incumbent Republican likely has to toe the line with Trump until then. 

That is, however, as long as Cassidy stays in the race, with multiple GOP sources indicating they expect him to do so, especially given his financial prowess. He must decide by the Feb. 13 filing deadline.

“If he doesn’t run again, you can probably kiss the second reconciliation bill goodbye immediately,” the senior GOP aide said. “If he becomes a ‘lean no’ vote when it comes down to it on the more controversial things, I think things grind to a halt starting in the summer.”

Cassidy, for his part, responded to Letlow’s announcement by writing on the social platform X, “I will continue to do a good job when I win re-election.”

Louisiana political strategist Mary-Patricia Wray, who’s worked with both Republicans and Democrats, said she spoke with the GOP senator at an energy industry event in October where she floated the idea of him running as an independent in the race, but she said the senator indicated he was not interested in pursuing it. Wray said she cut a small check to sponsor a lunch for Senate candidate Julie Emerson but hasn’t contributed money to any other candidates or endorsed a candidate in the Senate race.

Cassidy’s campaign did not respond to a request for comment for this story.

What also remains to be seen is whether the outside financial cavalry comes to the aid of either Cassidy or Letlow. 

The Senate Leadership Fund (SLF), which is run by Thune allies, indicated it may not play in the state as it is focused on the preservation and expansion of the Senate GOP, with Louisiana being considered a safe red seat. However, Trump’s decision to wade into the contest may force Republicans to spend in a state where their resources could have been used elsewhere. 

“Anything that distracts from our efforts to beat Democrats in November is unhelpful,” Alex Latcham, SLF executive director, said in a statement.

A second GOP operative also noted that the big question for Letlow is how much weight the Trump operation puts behind the endorsement and whether MAGA Inc. pumps funds into the race.  

The picture is especially complicated in Louisiana, where grassroots Republicans have soured on Cassidy ever since his vote to convict Trump. Some major donors, though, have rallied around the Republican senator — at least for now. 

“There’s a class of about 40 to 50 really wealthy donors in this state, and we all know who they are, and the president knows who they are, and they’re going to be expected to line up,” or at the very least start spreading around donations, said Republican consultant Scott Wilfong.

Cassidy has about $11 million in the bank as of the end of the year.

data.title: DOJ probe throws wrench into Trump's Fed plans
data.description:
The Justice Department’s criminal probe into the Federal Reserve is casting a shadow over President Trump’s plans for the central bank.

Trump is expected to announce his choice to succeed Fed Chair Jerome Powell within the next few weeks. But the threat of criminal charges against Powell may make it harder for Trump to replace the Fed chair and leave his mark on the bank.

Two GOP senators have already pledged to block Trump’s Fed nominees until the case against the bank is resolved. Trump’s eventual pick also could struggle to reassure senators and markets that they can be trusted to lead the Fed. 

And even if Trump’s pick makes it out of the Senate, they could face serious challenges imposing his agenda on a fractious Fed board facing unprecedented political pressure.

“The criminal investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is unlikely to influence the path for monetary policy, and could even backfire by making officials more reluctant to cut rates in the coming months and years,” wrote Bernard Yaros, lead U.S. economist at Oxford Economics, in an analysis last week.

Trump’s pressure hits new peak

Trump has been eager to remove Powell since 2018 over his refusal to cut interest rates to levels the president deems necessary, which were often well below the economic consensus.

The president has also griped that he should have more influence over the Fed, or at least be consulted on interest rate decisions that are supposed to be walled off from political influence.

Trump tried to impose his will on the Fed through a barrage of insults, insulting Powell’s intelligence and accusing the lifelong Republican of showing political bias toward Democrats through the bank’s interest rate decisions.

“I’d love to get the guy currently in there out right now, but people are holding me back,” Trump said in November ahead of a meeting with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman at the White House.

“He’s a fool. He’s a stupid man,” Trump added.

As Powell refused to yield, Trump turned his attention this summer to the Fed renovations as a potential pressure point. While the president seemed satisfied by Powell’s explanations during a tour of the renovations in July, Trump renewed threats to bring a lawsuit against the Fed later as he faced growing backlash on the economy.

Trump and the DOJ have insisted the Fed probe has nothing to do with the bank’s handling of interest rates. Trump also claimed to have no idea about the probe despite repeatedly threatening to sue Powell over the Fed’s renovations, frequently while the president was addressing the state of the economy.

But Fed experts, economists and many lawmakers — even those aligned with Trump or opposed to Powell for their own reasons — aren’t buying it.

“This represents a major escalation in an already fraught relationship between the White House and the Fed, and hurts the optics around central bank independence,” Yaros wrote.

Even so, Yaros warned that this “latest broadside on Fed independence is likely to prove ineffective and could even backfire on the administration.”

Steep Senate challenges

GOP Sens. Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) and Thom Tillis (N.C.) have vowed to oppose any Trump Fed nominee until the case against Powell resolved. As a member of the Senate Banking Committee, Tillis can also prevent the panel from approving the nominee, which could prevent or impede a full Senate confirmation vote.

“Tillis could be crucial in any vote to confirm a new Fed chair,” said Ian Katz, director at research firm Capital Alpha Partners.

“Tillis has long-held views about the importance of Fed independence, has defied Trump before and isn’t running for reelection,” he continued. “It’s unclear how many other Republican senators will stand with Tillis.”

The eventual Fed nominee will also face a storm of questions from other Banking panel members concerned with Trump’s plans for the Fed and eager for his new pick to prove their independence.

Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.), the top Democrat on the Senate Banking Committee, accused Trump on Friday of “waging an all-out war to seize control of the Fed.”

“I’ve long been a critic of the way the Federal Reserve does business. For far too long, the Fed has consistently favored Wall Street over Main Street,” Warren told reporters Friday.

Warren fiercely opposed former President Biden’s renomination of Powell in 2022, calling the Fed chair “a dangerous man” who failed to effectively oversee big banks.

“But I think we can all agree that the Fed works best, when its decisions are based on data, evidence, and what’s in the best interests of the American economy,” Warren said.

Trump’s chair would face old divides, new wrinkles

If Trump’s nominee can make it through the Senate, they could still be stonewalled by other Fed officials unwilling to bend to the president’s will.

The Fed chair does not have the power to raise or reduce interest rates on their own. Those decisions are made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), a panel of 12 bank officials comprising the Fed’s seven-person board of governors and rotation of five regional reserve bank presidents.

While the Fed chair wields influence over the FOMC as the panel’s chair, it cannot directly control, change or invalidate the outcome of the vote.

“The widespread backlash to the news reinforces our view that the Administration’s pick to be the next Fed Chair would be constrained in attempting to guide the rest of the FOMC toward approving a substantially lower policy rate than they otherwise would have seen as appropriate given economic conditions,” wrote LH Meyer Monetary Policy Analytics in a research note.

The FOMC has been unusually divided in recent months, with Fed officials split over whether sagging employment or plateauing inflation is the bigger threat to the economy. The panel voted to cut interest rates in December by a 9-3 margin, marking the first time since 2019 that more than two FOMC members dissented from the prevailing side.

“There is good reason to worry that a Trump loyalist as Chair would strong-arm the committee into much lower interest rates than economic conditions warrant, but we should also consider what happens if Trump’s Fed Chair is outvoted on the committee and can’t deliver on the promised cuts,” wrote Claudia Sahm, a former Fed research director. 

“We could be on the verge of a revolving door of Trump Fed Chairs, each less credible and less effective than the last.”

data.title: Donald Trump is a carpe diem president in a 'meet the moment' world
data.description:
Of all the buzz phrases obviously in need of retirement, “meet the moment” stands high on the list. No, Alyssa from accounting did not “meet the moment” with her Slack message, nor does some political celebrity (or celebrity politician) spouting some blather on social media.

The term, which seems to have sprung into popular use in the past five years or so, is a sort of left-coded version of the even more insipid right-wing phrase: that someone does or doesn’t “know what time it is.”

Both phrases suggest that actions otherwise forbidden are now necessary because of circumstances. Of course, sometimes, that’s really the case. Abraham Lincoln, the ultimate meeter of moments in American history, said it this way at the start of the second year of the Civil War: “As our case is new, we must think anew and act anew.”

So, it’s not coincidental that many of the people who want to “meet the moment” or tell you what time it is are the same ones who have been saying for a decade that America is at the brink of another civil war or that the republic has already fallen. In one of the cruel loops of human nature, by acting as if the imaginary catastrophe has already come, we excuse behaviors that bring the crisis into reality. 

But along the way, there will be contortions. For instance, one might end up saying something like, “The national emergency is avoiding the national emergency,” which is how Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent explained the legal basis for President Trump imposing a 10 percent tax on goods imported from Denmark and seven other NATO member nations as a punishment for the Danes’ refusal to sell Greenland to the United States.

Bessent’s problem is that he is trying to use 21st century reasoning — moment meeting and time telling — to a very 19th century way of thinking favored by his boss. Today, people don’t want to be seen as aggressors so much as they do victims of aggression who are fighting back. In the 1800s, leaders spoke with religious fervor about subjugating others or confiscating land. They spoke of Manifest Destiny.

Trump certainly knows the value of grievance. A leader who tells one of his counterparts that he is entitled to have another country’s holdings because he was snubbed for an award is certainly not above some motivated victimology.

But in Trump’s message to the prime minister of Norway, the president says something very much out of step with the times: “Considering your Country decided not to give me the Nobel Peace Prize for having stopped 8 Wars PLUS, I no longer feel an obligation to think purely of Peace, although it will always be predominant, but can now think about what is good and proper for the United States of America.”

In an era of “meet the moment,” Trump is going the other way. He’s all in on carpe diem, and says it’s time to seize the day — or whatever else — he wants and thinks is “good and proper” for the nation. 

In 1844, James Polk, once a rising star of the Jacksonian populist movement who had been embarrassed when he lost reelection as governor of Tennessee, was nobody’s favorite to win the Democratic nomination. But, two things alone propelled the dark and diminutive former Speaker of the House into the nomination and the presidency: statehood for Texas and westward expansion generally. 

Not even the most aggressive expansionists could have imagined what Polk had planned: to acquire all of what we now know as the continental United States, increasing the size of the nation by about 50 percent.

In addition to what is now Texas, Polk would bring in the territory that we now know as the states of California, Nevada, New Mexico, Arizona, Utah, Washington, Idaho, and Oregon, as well as parts of what would later become Oklahoma, Colorado, Kansas, Wyoming and Montana.

Polk would nearly break his party and sow the seeds of the sectional crisis that would lead to the Civil War. He started a war with Mexico on the most dubious grounds. He brawled and battled — but would not be denied. 

Trump lacks Polk’s self-discipline, but certainly he has his predecessor’s capacity for ruthlessness as well as a gift for shaping public opinion far beyond Polk. But wehat Trump lacks is a cause like Polk’s. While Manifest Destiny had its detractors, particularly among those who feared the growing power of the slave states, the idea of America as a continent-straddling colossus was part of our national identity since before the founding.

Thomas Jefferson’s Louisiana Purchase and Andrew Jackson’s Trail of Tears were both in service of the same idea that propelled settlers across the Appalachians, helping to trigger the Revolutionary War. The belief that this land had been granted by God was powerful stuff. Jefferson’s idea for the Great Seal of the United States depicted Moses leading the Children of Israel out of bondage and into the promised land. And what, after all, did the Israelites do with the Canaanites who stood in their way? 

It was Polk who finally completed that vision, and yet, he is now little remembered and mostly unloved. His disappearance from history is mostly owed to the unfashionability of his views and tactics. Americans came to like better the identity as liberators — the people who freed the slaves and twice conquered Western Europe and only kept enough land to bury our dead — than subjugators. The Europeans who launched wars and committed war crimes in the name of territorial domination became the barbarians while America became the arsenal of democracy.

Maybe that’s over now, and maybe Trump knows what time it is in a way that his opponents do not. But one suspects that long before Americans believe our destiny lies in Greenland, it will be the president and his party that are out in the cold.

data.title: GOP warnings over Greenland look unlikely to sway Trump
data.description:
Republican senators are bracing themselves for the potential fallout of President Trump’s aggressive bid to seize control of Greenland as the president prepares to meet with European leaders at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, on Wednesday.

Key GOP lawmakers have warned Trump to back away from his aggressive efforts to take control of Greenland, but their efforts have failed to make an impact.

The president over the weekend instead escalated the standoff with NATO allies by threatening steep tariffs on Germany, France, Britain and other European countries unless they move away from their opposition to the U.S. taking Greenland.

Two Republicans who are attending the economic forum in Davos are making it clear to European allies that Trump’s audacious efforts to ramp up pressure on Denmark and other European countries have divided the Republican Party in Washington.

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) on Tuesday called Trump’s push for Greenland “a waste of taxpayer dollars” and “drama that we don’t need” during an interview in Davos with CNBC’s “Squawk Box.”

Tillis said Trump needs an adviser who “understands the destabilizing influence on NATO” created by his unrelenting push to take control of Greenland.

Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska), who is also attending meetings in Davos, has called Trump’s threats against European allies “deeply troubling.”

She said the tariffs Trump announced against European trading partners were “unnecessary, punitive and a profound mistake.”

Trump is expected to have conversations with NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte while in Switzerland.

A Senate Republican aide who requested anonymity to comment on reactions from Republican lawmakers to Trump’s latest tariff threats questioned whether the president has the authority under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to impose tariffs on allies because they have supported Denmark’s refusal to simply cede its territory to the United States.

Greenland has long been a part of Denmark, and its citizens have not indicated a desire to become part of the United States.

The GOP aide argued Trump justified his first round of “Liberation Day” tariffs against Europe last year as being driven by an economic emergency but asserted that Trump’s bid for Greenland “has nothing to do with economics.”

“They sure haven’t laid out a very good case in the court of public opinion,” the aide said, arguing Trump hasn’t made a clear economic argument for taking control of Greenland.

The aide said Republican senators will watch closely to see what impact Trump’s threats are having on the financial markets and what additional steps he takes to achieve his goal of wresting Greenland from Danish control.

“We’ll see how much effect this will have on the market by the time everyone gets back [to Washington] and what practical steps the administration has taken,” the source said.

The major U.S. stock indices dropped by about 2 percent on Tuesday as investors digested Trump’s latest round of tariff threats. It marked the worst day of trading since October.

Trump appears undeterred by the backlash from European allies and some GOP lawmakers in Washington.

John Ullyot, a GOP strategist who served as a deputy assistant to Trump during his first term, said the president has made up his mind and predicted it’s unlikely anything will divert him from his focus on taking Greenland.

“President Trump has made up his mind, and when he makes up his mind, no one can talk him off of it. That’s been very good for him in so many ways politically for the last 10 years,” he said.

Ullyot said past presidents going back to Dwight D. Eisenhower, the nation’s 34th commander in chief, “have thought about the value of Greenland.”

“It’s even more important now with the rare earth [minerals] that weren’t a big factor 50, 70 years ago,” he said. “He put out exactly what he wants to do here and senators who are very pro-NATO are going to have heartburn with this, but it’s going to be hard for them to go against the president.

He predicted a bigger move from Trump to gain control of Greenland is “likely to happen in the short term.”

Ullyot said Trump “has already telegraphed that he’s willing to put the NATO alliance on the edge for wanting to get Greenland.”

Whit Ayres, a prominent Republican pollster, offered a different view. He said Trump’s all-out push to acquire Greenland has little public support in the United States and has divided the Republican Party.

“Based on the polling I’ve seen on the Greenland gambit, Sen. Tillis succinctly summed up public opinion when he said, ‘I’m sick of stupid.’ The support for aggressively going after Denmark to take control of Greenland has the support of maybe half of Trump’s base, at best,” Ayres said.

He noted that a recent Reuters/Ipsos poll found that just 4 percent of surveyed Americans, including just 1 in 10 Republicans, think it would be a “good idea” to use military force to take possession of Greenland.  

“You can get 4 percent of Americans in a survey to say they believe little green men live on the moon,” he quipped.

“Rarely does the public so widely agree on a foreign policy question as they agree on this Greenland gambit,” Ayres added, observing that a foreign policy issue has rarely been so elevated in the national political debate since the end of the Iraq War.

A second Senate GOP aide who requested anonymity to comment on sentiment within the GOP conference over Trump’s Greenland agenda said a lot will depend on whether the president succeeds in cowing allies.

The aide, however, said many Republicans aren’t happy about Trump renewing the trade war with European economic powers after tariff rates appeared to be settled after unveiling a deal in late July.

European leaders are now discussing the implementation of $108 billion in retaliatory tariffs on American products. Most worrisome for the U.S. stock markets, Europeans are mulling significant restrictions on American Big Tech companies, a drastic tactic that has been dubbed a trade “bazooka.”

Apple, which has significant business interests in Europe, saw its share price drop by 4 percent Tuesday.

Former Republican Leader Mitch McConnell (Ky.) is one of the loudest critics of Trump’s brinkmanship with NATO allies over Greenland.

He warned on the Senate floor that undermining NATO would harm U.S. national security interests far more than acquiring Greenland would help them. He said that “incinerating the hard-won trust of loyal allies” would result in an “unprecedented act of strategic self-harm.”

Former Sen. Judd Gregg (N.H.), who served on McConnell’s Senate GOP leadership team, said more Republican senators need to speak out against Trump’s aggression toward Greenland and NATO allies.

“A lot of them appear to be hiding under their desks, which is inexcusable. They should be speaking up aggressively and saying this is a terrible idea and it undermines our position with key allies,” he said of GOP senators who have shied from confronting Trump over his posture on Greenland, despite their personal misgivings.

Gregg pointed out that Denmark is willing to expand the U.S. military presence on Greenland to deter Chinese and Russian expansion into the Arctic, a point several sitting Republican senators have made over the past week.

“The fact is over the years we’ve gotten whatever we want in the way of military cooperation from Denmark and from Greenland. In fact, for years we had many bases there,” he said. “It’s not a military issue.”

Gregg said the president “claims it’s for bases, it’s really for his ego.”

He said Trump “likes to be the center of attention and he likes to stir things up.”

Gregg argued the fight with NATO allies over Greenland threatens to “undermine a lot of people’s confidence in American leadership on other issues, whether it’s trade or international authority.”

data.title: 5 things to know about Virginia's redistricting effort
data.description:
Virginia Democrats are moving to pass a new House map that will allow them to net a handful of seats ahead of the November midterms.

Democrats in the Old Dominion passed a constitutional amendment for a second time last week to allow the party to redraw the congressional lines by 2030.

The move comes as Florida Republicans are planning to convene in April for a special redistricting session, underscoring the urgency both parties face ahead of an election that will decide control of the House and Senate for President Trump’s last two years in office.

Here are five things to know about Virginia’s redistricting effort:

The decision will go before voters

The constitutional amendment passed by Virginia Democrats will now go before voters, allowing them to decide whether lawmakers can do mid-decade redistricting under a narrow set of circumstances.

The constitutional amendment would allow legislators to redraw Virginia’s map by October 2030 if another state has conducted mid-decade redistricting for any other reason besides responding to a court order or finishing the decennial redistricting process after the 2020 U.S. Census. It also would allow Democrats to temporarily bypass their redistricting commission.

Lawmakers passed the constitutional amendment twice — before and after the 2025 elections — and Democrats are eyeing an April statewide vote on the measure. Democrats also said they plan to have a congressional map finalized before the special election so voters can see what kind of map they’re weighing.

Democrats need a simple majority in the election to pass the constitutional amendment before the new maps can be put in place.

The map hasn’t been decided yet

Virginia Democrats are considering two different maps as the party weighs how aggressive it should be ahead of the midterms.

Democrats have a 6-5 edge in the state’s congressional delegation, but the party is eyeing either a 9-2 favored map or a 10-1 map. Some top Democrats have signaled they’re planning for the more aggressive 10-1 map.

“I said in August of 2025 that the maps will be 10-1 and I’m sticking with that today. Anyone in the Congressional delegation who wants a seat needs to campaign for it and not expect a safe seat,” Virginia Senate President Pro Tem L. Louise Lucas (D) wrote earlier this month.

Democrats have an incentive to pass a more aggressive map given that Florida Republicans are eying new maps, which could net up to five seats for the GOP. Yet it also creates more competitive congressional districts for some Democrats by expanding the map.

Democrats, however, are expected to see a favorable midterm environment in November, which could ease some of those concerns.

The party plans to decide which map it wants to use by the end of January. The candidate filing deadline for Virginia’s June primary is April 2, meaning Democrats will need to adjust the date in order for candidates to qualify under the new lines.

Democrats expect the effort to pass

Democrats feel confident that their redistricting measure will pass given Virginia’s blue leanings statewide.

Though there has been little public polling over redistricting in the Old Dominion, the party has been able to successfully convince voters to approve mid-decade redistricting elsewhere.

California Democrats were able to overwhelmingly pass their new House map, which aims to net the party up to five seats, in November. The party benefited from an unorganized and insufficiently funded opposition campaign in the Golden State.

Meanwhile, Virginia Democrats are coming out of a successful election in 2025, which saw them overperform in state legislative races, as well as the contest for governor.

GOP has sought to halt the effort

Republicans have tried unsuccessfully to halt Democrats’ effort to pass their redistricting measure multiple times. Most recently, a county judge last week denied a bid by the GOP to block the redistricting process.

“For well over a century, the courts of the Commonwealth have recognized a bedrock principle that amending the Constitution is a process left exclusively to the sound judgment of the Legislature that proposes amendments and the citizens that ratify or reject them,” the judge wrote.

“While that process is ongoing, ‘the courts cannot interfere to stop any of the proceedings,’” the judge added.

Republicans are expected to continue fighting the redistricting process, likely through an opposition campaign organized against the ballot measure. However, the party could try to thwart the process in order ways, such as by contesting the ballot language.

Republicans faced a similar setback in California after a federal panel of judges rejected a bid last week to halt the Golden State’s new congressional map from going into effect before November.

Democrats were also unsuccessful at barring Texas from using its new GOP-favored map in time for the 2026 midterms.

It’s Dems’ last big opportunity to gain seats

The stakes of Virginia’s redistricting effort are high because it’s seen as one of Democrats’ last assured opportunities to redraw new congressional lines before November.

In Maryland, where Democrats hold a 7-1 edge, Gov. Wes Moore’s (D) Redistricting Advisory Commission is set to vote on a new congressional map proposal, though it still requires legislative approval. The party is looking to weaken Rep. Andy Harris’s (R-Md.) district in the Eastern Shore. Democrats tried once before to create an aggressive 8-0 map after the 2020 U.S. Census, only to see it shot down as a partisan gerrymander by a court.

Maryland Senate President Bill Ferguson (D) has also been vocally opposed to drawing a new map, raising questions of whether Democrats even have the numbers in both chambers to pass a new map. If they do, it’s not clear it would withstand litigation from Republicans again.

Florida’s expected special session on redistricting in April also is adding to the urgency for Democrats.

However, Republicans must navigate a few hurdles of their own, including restrictive anti-gerrymandering language included in the state Constitution and potentially differing timelines between Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) and Florida House Republicans of when to draw a new map.
